---
title: "About"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
---

## Citing theoryforge

If you use the R package in your work, please cite it:

<p style="margin: 0 0 0.6em 0; padding-left: 1.8em; text-indent: -1.8em;">Bernabeu, P. (2026). <em>theoryforge: Systematic theory development</em> (R package version `r utils::packageVersion("theoryforge")`). <a href="https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21229964">https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21229964</a></p>

```{r bibtex-entry, echo = FALSE, results = "asis"}
# The code fence is assembled at run time; a literal fence line inside the
# chunk would terminate it early.
fence <- strrep("`", 3)
cat(sprintf('%sbibtex
@Manual{theoryforge,
  title  = {theoryforge: Systematic theory development},
  author = {Pablo Bernabeu},
  year   = {2026},
  note   = {R package version %s},
  doi    = {10.5281/zenodo.21229964},
  url    = {https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21229964},
}
%s
', fence, utils::packageVersion("theoryforge"), fence))
```

You can <a href="../theoryforge.bib" download>download the BibTeX entry</a>. In R, the
same entry is available with `citation("theoryforge")`. Once on CRAN, the package will
install with `install.packages("theoryforge")`; until then, use
`remotes::install_github("pablobernabeu/theoryforge", subdir = "r/theoryforge")`.

## The developer

theoryforge is developed and maintained by
[Pablo Bernabeu](https://pablobernabeu.github.io), a researcher in the
Department of Education at the University of Oxford. His work spans cognitive psychology and
neuroscience, linguistics, education and digital technologies, and research methods and open
science, with hands-on experience in behavioural and EEG experiments, corpus analysis,
computational modelling and statistics. He develops open, reproducible research software in R
and Python, and is a Fellow of the Software Sustainability Institute. His
[ORCID record](https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1083-2460) lists his other work.

This R package has a feature-parity twin written in Python. Its documentation lives at the
[theoryforge (Python) site](https://pablobernabeu.github.io/theoryforge/python/), keeping a
theory development workflow legible across both languages.

## Licence

theoryforge is released under the MIT licence, a permissive licence that allows use,
modification and redistribution provided the copyright and licence notices are kept. The full
text is reproduced on this site's [licence page](../LICENSE.html).

## Versioning and archival

Releases are tagged on [GitHub](https://github.com/pablobernabeu/theoryforge/releases) and
archived on [Zenodo](https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21229964). The concept DOI,
10.5281/zenodo.21229964, always resolves to the latest version, so a citation stays current
without naming a version. The [changelog](../news/index.html) records what changed in each
release.

## Contributing and support

Bugs and feature requests are best reported on the
[GitHub issues page](https://github.com/pablobernabeu/theoryforge/issues), and the
[contributing guide](../CONTRIBUTING.html) explains how to set up a development environment
and propose a change. Because the OSF deposit adapter takes a personal access token, never
paste that token, or any other secret, into an issue; replace it with a placeholder.
